title: Progressive Growing: Step-by-Step
concept: Progressive GAN starts by training on tiny 4x4 images, then gradually adds layers to upscale to 8x8, 16x16, and eventually 1024x1024 as training progresses.

exercises.explain: Before diving into progressive growing, remember that standard GANs try to generate the final high-resolution image all at once, which is incredibly difficult to stabilize.
exercises.prompt: Why is training standard GANs on high-resolution images so difficult?

options.text: They only generate black and white images first.
They try to generate high-resolution details from the very beginning.

cards.title: The Blank Canvas Struggle
cards.body: Imagine drawing a hyper-detailed masterpiece on your first try without sketching. Sounds impossible, right? Traditional GANs fail because they try to generate massive, detailed 1024x1024 images right from the start!

cards.title: Step-by-Step Growth
cards.body: Progressive Growing is a training technique where we start by training the GAN on tiny, low-resolution images (like 4x4 pixels). Once it masters those, we slowly add new layers to the Generator and Discriminator to double the resolution to 8x8, 16x16, and eventually 1024x1024.

Think of Progressive GANs like learning to paint. You don't start with a giant canvas detailing every single brushstroke of a face. You start with a tiny rough sketch to get the basic shapes down, then grab a medium canvas for outlines, and finally zoom in on a giant canvas for details like eyelashes.
cards.title: The Portrait Artist

cards.body: In practice, a Progressive GAN starts by learning to generate blurry 4x4 blobs that vaguely look like a face shape. As layers are added, those blobs become 16x16 pixel shapes with eyes, and finally transform into a crisp 1024x1024 photo-realistic portrait.
cards.title: From Blobs to Beauty
No Shock to the System
cards.body: You might think adding new layers instantly ruins the training because they are brand new. However, we fade the new layers in gradually using a blend factor so the network doesn't get shocked by the sudden change in resolution.

right.text: Starts at a simple 4x4 resolution, adding detail layers only when ready.

left.text: Tries to jump straight to generating complex 1024x1024 details, often failing.

exercises.explain: Starting with a small 4x4 resolution allows the network to learn the overall structure of the image before worrying about fine details like hair or skin texture.

options.text: It lets the model master the basic shape and structure before adding fine details.
It forces the model to ignore color entirely.
It reduces the amount of training data we need.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary benefit of starting a Progressive GAN at a tiny 4x4 resolution?

exercises.explain: True! A Progressive GAN adds layers step-by-step, doubling the resolution each time (4x4, 8x8, 16x16...) as training goes on.
exercises.prompt: Progressive GANs grow the network size during training rather than keeping it constant.
Complete the description of Progressive GAN training:
exercises.sentence: The network starts training on very small [blank] images before adding layers.
exercises.explain: Progressive GAN training begins at the extremely low resolution of 4x4 pixels, then grows from there.

options.text: A high-definition 1024x1024 portrait of a person who doesn't exist, built up from 4x4 blobs.
An algorithm that compresses a 4K video into a tiny thumbnail.

exercises.explain: Progressive GANs are famous for generating high-resolution, photorealistic human faces that look incredibly detailed.
exercises.prompt: Which scenario demonstrates a Progressive GAN in action?
How do the resolutions change during Progressive GAN training?

options.text: Start at 1024x1024 and remove layers to shrink down to 4x4.
Start at 4x4, then gradually add layers to train at 8x8, 16x16, and so on.
Train only the generator on 4x4 and the discriminator on 1024x1024.

exercises.explain: The generator and discriminator grow hand-in-hand. We start with 4x4, then transition to 8x8, 16x16, and keep doubling layers until we reach the target high resolution (like 1024x1024). Next, we will explore the math behind fading in these new layers smoothly!
